Love will be a commanding, constraining principle; and where love is, duty follows from a principle of gratitude. God will not only love obedient believers, but he will take pleasure in loving them, will rest in love to them. He will be with them as his home. These privileges are confined to those whose faith worketh by love, and whose love to Jesus leads them to keep his commandments. Such are partakers of the Holy Spirit's new-creating grace.
14.m:
Christ promised to always look after his followers. I won’t abandon you, I’ll stay close even if I’m gone. I’ll return quickly after my resurrection. I’ll visit you through my Spirit every day. I’ll come back again at the end of time. Only those who believe in me now will see me forever. The world won’t see me until my second coming, but my followers will have fellowship with me while I’m away. We’ll understand these things completely in heaven.

It’s a gift that you get to know about this and find comfort in it. When you have my rules, follow them. Knowing my commands isn’t enough—you need to live by them too. True love for me shows up as obeying my teachings. All true believers receive signs of my presence and love. Sincere love for Christ results in following his commands. Love motivates us to do what’s right out of thankfulness. God loves faithful believers and takes joy in their devotion. He makes himself at home with those who love him.

These blessings belong only to those whose faith comes from love and whose love guides them to keep my commandments. People who show this kind of love are changed by the Holy Spirit’s power.

14.o: Would we know these things for our good, we must pray for, and depend on the teaching of the Holy Ghost; thus the words of Jesus will be brought to our remembrance, and many difficulties be cleared up which are not plain to others. To all the saints, the Spirit of grace is given to be a remembrancer, and to him, by faith and prayer, we should commit the keeping of what we hear and know. Peace is put for all good, and Christ has left us all that is really and truly good, all the promised good; peace of mind from our justification before God. This Christ calls his peace, for he is himself our Peace. The peace of God widely differs from that of Pharisees or hypocrites, as is shown by its humbling and holy effects.
14.m:
To understand these things well, we need to pray and rely on the guidance of the Holy Spirit. Then Jesus' teachings will come back to our minds, and many confusing parts will become clearer. The Holy Spirit helps believers remember important truths. We should trust in Him through faith and prayer to keep what we learn.

Peace means all true goodness, including inner peace because of being right with God. Jesus calls this His peace since He brings harmony. True peace from God is different from the kind hypocrites have. It makes people humble and holy.

14.o: Christ raises the expectations of his disciples to something beyond what they thought was their greatest happiness. His time was now short, he therefore spake largely to them. When we come to be sick, and to die, we may not be capable of talking much to those about us; such good counsel as we have to give, let us give while in health. Observe the prospect Christ had of an approaching conflict, not only with men, but with the powers of darkness. Satan has something in us to perplex us with, for we have all sinned; but when he would disturb Christ, he found nothing sinful to help him. The best evidence of our love to the Father is, our doing as he has commanded us. Let us rejoice in the Saviour's victories over Satan the prince of this world. Let us copy the example of his love and obedience.
14.m:
Jesus raised his followers' hopes for something better than they ever imagined. Since his time was limited, he talked openly with them. 

When we get sick or near death, we might not be able to talk much to others. So, we should share important advice while we're healthy.

Notice Jesus knew he faced a difficult battle ahead—not just against people, but also against evil forces. 

Satan tries to trouble us because we all sin. But when trying to bother Jesus, he couldn't find anything wrong in him. 

The best way to show we love God is by following his commands. We should celebrate Jesus defeating Satan, who rules this world. And we should follow Jesus’ example of loving and obeying God.

15.o: Jesus Christ is the Vine, the true Vine. The union of the human and Divine natures, and the fulness of the Spirit that is in him, resemble the root of the vine made fruitful by the moisture from a rich soil. Believers are branches of this Vine. The root is unseen, and our life is hid with Christ; the root bears the tree, diffuses sap to it, and in Christ are all supports and supplies. The branches of the vine are many, yet, meeting in the root, are all but one vine; thus all true Christians, though in place and opinion distant from each other, meet in Christ. Believers, like the branches of the vine, are weak, and unable to stand but as they are borne up. The Father is the Husbandman. Never was any husbandman so wise, so watchful, about his vineyard, as God is about his church, which therefore must prosper. We must be fruitful. From a vine we look for grapes, and from a Christian we look for a Christian temper, disposition, and life. We must honour God, and do good; this is bearing fruit. The unfruitful are taken away. And even fruitful branches need pruning; for the best have notions, passions, and humours, that require to be taken away, which Christ has promised to forward the sanctification of believers, they will be thankful, for them. The word of Christ is spoken to all believers; and there is a cleansing virtue in that word, as it works grace, and works out corruption. And the more fruit we bring forth, the more we abound in what is good, the more our Lord is glorified. In order to fruitfulness, we must abide in Christ, must have union with him by faith. It is the great concern of all Christ's disciples, constantly to keep up dependence upon Christ, and communion with him. True Christians find by experience, that any interruption in the exercise of their faith, causes holy affections to decline, their corruptions to revive, and their comforts to droop. Those who abide not in Christ, though they may flourish for awhile in outward profession, yet come to nothing. The fire is the fittest place for withered branches; they are good for nothing else. Let us seek to live more simply on the fulness of Christ, and to grow more fruitful in every good word and work, so may our joy in Him and in his salvation be full.
15.m:
Jesus Christ is the Vine, the true Vine. His human nature joined with his divine nature, and the fullness of the Holy Spirit within him, is like the root of the vine that becomes fruitful thanks to water from rich soil. Believers are branches of this Vine. 

The root is hidden, just like our lives are hidden with Christ. The root holds up the whole plant and sends sap through its branches. So in Christ, we receive support and help. There are many branches on the vine, but they all connect at the root, making one vine. Likewise, all true Christians, even if far apart in location or belief, are united in Christ. 

Believers, like vine branches, are weak and cannot stand without being supported. The Father is the Gardener. He takes better care of his garden than any gardener would take of a vineyard. Because of this, his church prospers. We must produce fruit. Just as we expect grapes from a vine, people expect godly behavior from a Christian. This means honoring God and doing good deeds. Unfruitful branches get removed. Even those who bear fruit sometimes need pruning because everyone has bad thoughts and habits that need to go. But Jesus promises to help believers grow spiritually. They should be grateful for this.

God’s Word spoken to believers has power to create goodness and remove sin. As we produce more fruit, we show more of what is good, and this brings glory to our Lord. To be fruitful, we must stay connected to Christ through faith. For all of Christ’s followers, keeping close ties with him is very important. True believers know that when they stop trusting in Christ, their spiritual feelings weaken, their sins return, and their joy fades. People who don’t stay connected to Christ might seem fine for a while, but eventually they fade away. Withered branches belong in the fire—they serve no other purpose.

Let us try to live more simply by relying on all that Christ has for us. And let us grow more productive in doing good things. Then our joy in him and in his salvation will be complete.

15.o: Those whom God loves as a Father, may despise the hatred of all the world. As the Father loved Christ, who was most worthy, so he loved his disciples, who were unworthy. All that love the Saviour should continue in their love to him, and take all occasions to show it. The joy of the hypocrite is but for a moment, but the joy of those who abide in Christ's love is a continual feast. They are to show their love to him by keeping his commandments. If the same power that first shed abroad the love of Christ's in our hearts, did not keep us in that love, we should not long abide in it. Christ's love to us should direct us to love each other. He speaks as about to give many things in charge, yet names this only; it includes many duties.
15.m: God’s beloved children shouldn’t fear being hated by everyone else. Just as God loved Jesus, who was perfect, God also loved his followers, even though they weren't perfect. Anyone who truly loves Jesus should stay loyal to him and find ways to show it. Hypocrites might feel happy briefly, but true believers have lasting joy because they remain close to Jesus. To prove our love for Jesus, we need to follow his commands. Without God’s help, which gave us love for Jesus in the first place, we wouldn’t be able to keep loving him. Our love for Jesus should lead us to love others too. Even though there are many things Jesus wants to teach us, he mentions just one here. This single instruction covers many responsibilities.

15.o: How little do many persons think, that in opposing the doctrine of Christ as our Prophet, Priest, and King, they prove themselves ignorant of the one living and true God, whom they profess to worship! The name into which Christ's disciples were baptized, is that which they will live and die by. It is a comfort to the greatest sufferers, if they suffer for Christ's name's sake. The world's ignorance is the true cause of its hatred to the disciples of Jesus. The clearer and fuller the discoveries of the grace and truth of Christ, the greater is our sin if we do not love him and believe in him.
15.m:
Many people don't realize that when they disagree with what Jesus taught about being our teacher, priest, and king, they show they don’t really know the true God they claim to worship. 

The name given to Jesus' followers is the name they will be known by forever. 

It’s comforting for those who suffer if they are suffering because of their faith in Jesus.

People hate Jesus’ followers because they don’t understand them.

The more clearly Jesus shows his kindness and truth, the bigger our mistake becomes if we don’t love and trust him.

15.o: The blessed Spirit will maintain the cause of Christ in the world, notwithstanding the opposition it meets with. Believers taught and encouraged by his influences, would bear testimony to Christ and his salvation.
15.m: The Holy Spirit will keep up the cause of Christ in the world, even though there is opposition. With the Spirit’s help, believers will teach others about Christ and his salvation.

16.o: Our Lord Jesus, by giving his disciples notice of trouble, designed that the terror might not be a surprise to them. It is possible for those who are real enemies to God's service, to pretend zeal for it. This does not lessen the sin of the persecutors; villanies will never be changed by putting the name of God to them. As Jesus in his sufferings, so his followers in theirs, should look to the fulfilling of Scripture. He did not tell them sooner, because he was with them to teach, guide, and comfort them; they needed not then this promise of the Holy Spirit's presence. It will silence us to ask, Whence troubles come? It will satisfy us to ask, Whither go they? for we know they work for good. It is the common fault and folly of melancholy Christians to look only on the dark side of the cloud, and to turn a deaf ear to the voice of joy and gladness. That which filled the disciples' hearts with sorrow, was too great affection for this present life. Nothing more hinders our joy in God, than the love of the world, and the sorrow of the world which comes from it.
16.m:
Jesus told his disciples about future trouble so they wouldn't be surprised by it. Some people who really hate God’s work try to hide their true feelings by saying they support it. But pretending doesn’t make their actions less sinful. When Jesus suffered, he looked at what the Scriptures said would happen. And just like him, his followers should see their troubles as part of God’s plan.

He didn’t warn them earlier because he was there to teach, guide, and comfort them. They didn’t need the promise of the Holy Spirit yet. Asking where our troubles come from won’t help much. Instead, asking where they lead can bring peace, since we know they end up doing good things.

It’s a mistake for sad believers to focus only on the bad parts of life and ignore the joyful messages from God. The disciples were upset because they loved this world too much. Loving the world and its sorrows stops us from finding joy in God.

16.o: Christ's departure was necessary to the Comforter's coming. Sending the Spirit was to be the fruit of Christ's death, which was his going away. His bodily presence could be only in one place at one time, but his Spirit is every where, in all places, at all times, wherever two or three are gathered together in his name. See here the office of the Spirit, first to reprove, or to convince. Convincing work is the Spirit's work; he can do it effectually, and none but he. It is the method the Holy Spirit takes, first to convince, and then to comfort. The Spirit shall convince the world, of sin; not merely tell them of it. The Spirit convinces of the fact of sin; of the fault of sin; of the folly of sin; of the filth of sin, that by it we are become hateful to God; of the fountain of sin, the corrupt nature; and lastly, of the fruit of sin, that the end thereof is death. The Holy Spirit proves that all the world is guilty before God. He convinces the world of righteousness; that Jesus of Nazareth was Christ the righteous. Also, of Christ's righteousness, imparted to us for justification and salvation. He will show them where it is to be had, and how they may be accepted as righteous in God's sight. Christ's ascension proves the ransom was accepted, and the righteousness finished, through which believers were to be justified. Of judgment, because the prince of this world is judged. All will be well, when his power is broken, who made all the mischief. As Satan is subdued by Christ, this gives us confidence, for no other power can stand before him. And of the day of judgment. The coming of the Spirit would be of unspeakable advantage to the disciples. The Holy Spirit is our Guide, not only to show us the way, but to go with us by continued aids and influences. To be led into a truth is more than barely to know it; it is not only to have the notion of it in our heads, but the relish, and savour, and power of it in our hearts. He shall teach all truth, and keep back nothing profitable, for he will show things to come. All the gifts and graces of the Spirit, all the preaching, and all the writing of the apostles, under the influence of the Spirit, all the tongues, and miracles, were to glorify Christ. It behoves every one to ask, whether the Holy Spirit has begun a good work in his heart? Without clear discovery of our guilt and danger, we never shall understand the value of Christ's salvation; but when brought to know ourselves aright, we begin to see the value of the Redeemer. We should have fuller views of the Redeemer, and more lively affections to him, if we more prayed for, and depended on the Holy Spirit.
16.m:
Christ needed to leave so the Helper could come. Sending the Spirit was part of what Christ did after dying. When Christ left, his body couldn't be in many places at once, but his Spirit could be everywhere, always. 

The Spirit’s job is to show people their mistakes. Only the Spirit can do this well. First, the Spirit shows people their sins—what they’ve done wrong, how foolish those actions are, and how sinful they've become. Then, the Spirit shows them that Jesus was the Righteous One, and that his righteousness can save them. This means believers can be seen as good in God’s eyes.

When Christ went up to heaven, it showed that his sacrifice was accepted and that his work was complete. The Spirit also shows people about judgment, telling them that the devil has been defeated. This gives us hope because no other power can beat Christ. 

Also, the Spirit tells people about the final judgment. Having the Spirit helps the followers of Christ greatly. The Spirit guides them, showing them the right path and helping them stay on it. Learning from the Spirit isn’t just understanding ideas—it’s feeling and living out those truths deeply. The Spirit teaches everything useful and doesn’t hide anything helpful. He even reveals future events.

All the special abilities and virtues given by the Spirit, all the teachings and writings of the apostles guided by the Spirit, all their speaking different languages and doing miracles, were meant to honor Christ. 

Everyone should wonder if the Holy Spirit has started working inside them. Unless we clearly see our wrongdoings and dangers, we won’t truly appreciate Christ’s salvation. But once we learn who we really are, we start to see how valuable the Savior is.

We would better understand the Redeemer and feel stronger love for him if we asked more for the Holy Spirit’s help and trusted in Him more.

16.o: It is good to consider how near our seasons of grace are to an end, that we may be quickened to improve them. But the sorrows of the disciples would soon be turned into joy; as those of a mother, at the sight of her infant. The Holy Spirit would be their Comforter, and neither men nor devils, neither sufferings in life nor in death, would ever deprive them of their joy. Believers have joy or sorrow, according to their sight of Christ, and the tokens of his presence. Sorrow is coming on the ungodly, which nothing can lessen; the believer is an heir to joy which no one can take away. Where now is the joy of the murderers of our Lord, and the sorrow of his friends?
16.m:
It’s important to think about how close our times of God’s favor might come to ending so we use them well. But the sadness of Jesus’ followers would soon turn to happiness, just like a mother feels joy after seeing her baby. The Holy Spirit would comfort them, and nothing—neither people nor demons, suffering in life or death—could take their joy away. 

Believers feel happy or sad depending on whether they see Christ and know he is there. Those who don’t believe in God will face sorrow that cannot be lessened. But believers receive joy that no one can take from them. Where is the happiness of those who killed our Lord now? And where is the sorrow of his friends?

16.o: Asking of the Father shows a sense of spiritual wants, and a desire of spiritual blessings, with conviction that they are to be had from God only. Asking in Christ's name, is acknowledging our unworthiness to receive any favours from God, and shows full dependence upon Christ as the Lord our Righteousness. Our Lord had hitherto spoken in short and weighty sentences, or in parables, the import of which the disciples did not fully understand, but after his resurrection he intended plainly to teach them such things as related to the Father and the way to him, through his intercession. And the frequency with which our Lord enforces offering up petitions in his name, shows that the great end of the mediation of Christ is to impress us with a deep sense of our sinfulness, and of the merit and power of his death, whereby we have access to God. And let us ever remember, that to address the Father in the name of Christ, or to address the Son as God dwelling in human nature, and reconciling the world to himself, are the same, as the Father and Son are one.
16.m:
Asking the Father shows you know what you need spiritually and want blessings from God. It also means you believe God alone can give these things. 

When you ask in Jesus' name, you admit you don't deserve anything good from God on your own. This shows you depend completely on Jesus for everything.

Before, Jesus taught using short, powerful statements or stories that were hard to understand right away. But after rising from the dead, he wanted to clearly explain important truths about God the Father and how to reach him through Jesus.

Jesus often stressed asking in his name because it helps us realize how sinful we are and how much we need his help. His work makes it possible for us to talk to God.

Remember, talking to God the Father in Jesus' name is the same as talking to Jesus who became human to bring us back to God. The Father and Son are one.

16.o: Here is a plain declaration of Christ's coming from the Father, and his return to him. The Redeemer, in his entrance, was God manifest in the flesh, and in his departure was received up into glory. By this saying the disciples improved in knowledge. Also in faith; “Now are we sure.” Alas! they knew not their own weakness. The Divine nature did not desert the human nature, but supported it, and put comfort and value into Christ's sufferings. And while we have God's favourable presence, we are happy, and ought to be easy, though all the world forsake us. Peace in Christ is the only true peace, in him alone believers have it. Through him we have peace with God, and so in him we have peace in our own minds. We ought to be encouraged, because Christ has overcome the world before us. But while we think we stand, let us take heed lest we fall. We know not how we should act if brought into temptation; let us watch and pray without ceasing, that we may not be left to ourselves.
16.m: Here is a clear statement about Jesus coming from the Father and returning to him. When the Savior came, he was God made human, and when he went back, he was taken up to heaven. This helped the followers learn more. They said, "We now believe." Sadly, they didn't realize how weak they were. God’s spirit stayed with Jesus, giving strength and worth to his suffering. As long as we have God’s good presence, we are happy even if everyone else leaves us. True peace comes from being at one with Christ. Only through him do we find peace with God and inner peace too. We should feel confident knowing Christ already won over the world for us. Yet, we need to stay careful, because we might slip if we get too comfortable. We don’t know how we would handle big tests, so we must always watch and pray to avoid falling.

17.o: Our Lord prayed as a man, and as the Mediator of his people; yet he spoke with majesty and authority, as one with and equal to the Father. Eternal life could not be given to believers, unless Christ, their Surety, both glorified the Father, and was glorified of him. This is the sinner's way to eternal life, and when this knowledge shall be made perfect, holiness and happiness will be fully enjoyed. The holiness and happiness of the redeemed, are especially that glory of Christ, and of his Father, which was the joy set before him, for which he endured the cross and despised the shame; this glory was the end of the sorrow of his soul, and in obtaining it he was fully satisfied. Thus we are taught that our glorifying God is needed as an evidence of our interest in Christ, through whom eternal life is God's free gift.
17.m:
Jesus prayed like any person, but he spoke with power and authority because he was equal to the Father. Only after Jesus, who acted as our surety, honored the Father and was honored by him, could believers receive eternal life. 

This is the path sinners take to gain eternal life. When this understanding becomes complete, true holiness and happiness will be fully realized. The holiness and happiness of those saved bring great honor to both Christ and his Father. This glory was what gave Jesus strength to endure the suffering on the cross. It brought him satisfaction and comfort.

We learn from this that showing God honor is proof of our connection to Christ, who gives us eternal life freely.

17.o: Christ prays for those that are his. Thou gavest them me, as sheep to the shepherd, to be kept; as a patient to the physician, to be cured; as children to a tutor, to be taught: thus he will deliver up his charge. It is a great satisfaction to us, in our reliance upon Christ, that he, all he is and has, and all he said and did, all he is doing and will do, are of God. Christ offered this prayer for his people alone as believers; not for the world at large. Yet no one who desires to come to the Father, and is conscious that he is unworthy to come in his own name, need be discouraged by the Saviour's declaration, for he is both able and willing to save to the uttermost, all that come unto God by him. Earnest convictions and desires, are hopeful tokens of a work already wrought in a man; they begin to evidence that he has been chosen unto salvation, through sanctification of the Spirit and belief of the truth. They are thine; wilt thou not provide for thine own? Wilt thou not secure them? Observe the foundation on which this plea is grounded, All mine are thine, and thine are mine. This speaks the Father and Son to be one. All mine are thine. The Son owns none for his, that are not devoted to the service of the Father.
17.m:
Jesus prayed for his followers. You gave them to me, just like you would give sheep to a shepherd, patients to a doctor, or children to a teacher. He takes care of what belongs to him. 

It makes us feel good to know that everything Jesus is and does comes from God. When Jesus prayed, he only asked for those who believe in him. But anyone who wants to come to God and knows they aren’t worthy enough to ask on their own doesn't have to worry. Jesus is ready and able to help everyone who turns to him.

When someone feels bad about sinning and really wants to turn to God, it shows that God might already be working in their heart. These feelings start to show that God chose them for salvation through the Holy Spirit and faith in the truth.

They belong to you. Will you take care of them? Notice the reason behind this request: All my followers are yours, and all your followers are mine. This shows that the Father and Son are one. Everything I claim as mine is also yours, and everything you claim as yours is also mine.

17.o: Christ does not pray that they might be rich and great in the world, but that they might be kept from sin, strengthened for their duty, and brought safe to heaven. The prosperity of the soul is the best prosperity. He pleaded with his holy Father, that he would keep them by his power and for his glory, that they might be united in affection and labours, even according to the union of the Father and the Son. He did not pray that his disciples should be removed out of the world, that they might escape the rage of men, for they had a great work to do for the glory of God, and the benefit of mankind. But he prayed that the Father would keep them from the evil, from being corrupted by the world, the remains of sin in their hearts, and from the power and craft of Satan. So that they might pass through the world as through an enemy's country, as he had done. They are not left here to pursue the same objects as the men around them, but to glorify God, and to serve their generation. The Spirit of God in true Christians is opposed to the spirit of the world.
17.m:
Christ didn’t ask that his followers become rich or famous in the world. Instead, he asked that they stay away from sin, grow strong in doing what’s right, and reach heaven safely. 

He talked to his heavenly Father, asking him to protect his followers and use them for his honor. Christ wanted them to work together closely, just like the Father and Son do. 

He didn’t pray that his followers would leave the world to avoid trouble from people who hated them. They had important jobs to do for God’s glory and to help others. 

Instead, he asked the Father to guard them from bad influences—both the sins still in their hearts and the tricks of Satan. This way, they could live in the world without being harmed by it, just as Jesus himself did. 

They were sent here to focus on serving God and helping others, not to chase after the same things everyone else wants. The Holy Spirit inside real believers fights against the ways of the world.

17.o: Christ next prayed for the disciples, that they might not only be kept from evil, but made good. It is the prayer of Jesus for all that are his, that they may be made holy. Even disciples must pray for sanctifying grace. The means of giving this grace is, “through thy truth, thy word is truth.” Sanctify them, set them apart for thyself and thy service. Own them in the office; let thy hand go with them. Jesus entirely devoted himself to his undertaking, and all the parts of it, especially the offering up himself without spot unto God, by the eternal Spirit. The real holiness of all true Christians is the fruit of Christ's death, by which the gift of the Holy Ghost was purchased; he gave himself for his church, to sanctify it. If our views have not this effect on us, they are not Divine truth, or we do not receive them by a living and a working faith, but as mere notions.
17.m:
Christ then prayed for his followers, asking that they would not just be protected from bad things, but also become good. This is what Jesus prays for everyone who belongs to him—sanctification, making them holy. Disciples need to ask for this kind of grace too. 

The way to give this grace is through "thy truth," because "thy word is truth." Make them holy, separate them for yourself and your work. Claim them as yours. Jesus fully dedicated himself to his mission, including sacrificing himself without sin, led by the eternal Spirit. 

True Christians' real holiness comes from Christ’s death, which bought the gift of the Holy Spirit. He gave himself to sanctify his church. If hearing about these truths doesn’t change us, then either those aren't divine truths, or we’re not receiving them with a living, active faith.

17.o: Our Lord especially prayed, that all believers might be as one body under one head, animated by one soul, by their union with Christ and the Father in him, through the Holy Spirit dwelling in them. The more they dispute about lesser things, the more they throw doubts upon Christianity. Let us endeavour to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace, praying that all believers may be more and more united in one mind and one judgment. Thus shall we convince the world of the truth and excellence of our religion, and find more sweet communion with God and his saints.
17.m:
Our Lord Jesus asked that all believers would be united, like parts of one body connected to one head. They should be joined together by their relationship with Christ and the Father, guided by the Holy Spirit living inside them. 

When people argue over small details, they often question whether Christianity is true. We need to work hard to stay united in peace, praying for all believers to agree on important matters. This will show the world how good and true our faith is. It will also help us enjoy closer fellowship with God and each other.

17.o: Christ, as one with the Father, claimed on behalf of all that had been given to him, and should in due time believe on him, that they should be brought to heaven; and that there the whole company of the redeemed might behold his glory as their beloved Friend and Brother, and therein find happiness. He had declared and would further declare the name or character of God, by his doctrine and his Spirit, that, being one with him, the love of the Father to him might abide with them also. Thus, being joined to Him by one Spirit, they might be filled with all the fulness of God, and enjoy a blessedness of which we can form no right idea in our present state.
17.m:
Jesus, who was one with the Father, said that everyone who believes in him will go to heaven. There, those saved people will see Jesus' glory and be happy. 

He would tell others about God’s nature through his teachings and the Holy Spirit. Since he is united with the Father, the Father’s love for him would stay with believers too. By connecting to Jesus through the Spirit, they could experience all of God’s fullness and joy. We cannot fully imagine what that kind of happiness is like now.

18.o: Sin began in the garden of Eden, there the curse was pronounced, there the Redeemer was promised; and in a garden that promised Seed entered into conflict with the old serpent. Christ was buried also in a garden. Let us, when we walk in our gardens, take occasion from thence to mediate on Christ's sufferings in a garden. Our Lord Jesus, knowing all things that should come upon him, went forth and asked, Whom seek ye? When the people would have forced him to a crown, he withdrew, ch. 6:15, but when they came to force him to a cross, he offered himself; for he came into this world to suffer, and went to the other world to reign. He showed plainly what he could have done; when he struck them down he could have struck them dead, but he would not do so. It must have been the effect of Divine power, that the officers and soldiers let the disciples go away quietly, after the resistance which had been offered. Christ set us an example of meekness in sufferings, and a pattern of submission to God's will in every thing that concerns us. It is but a cup, a small matter. It is a cup that is given us; sufferings are gifts. It is given us by a Father, who has a father's authority, and does us no wrong; a father's affection, and means us no hurt. From the example of our Saviour we should learn how to receive our lighter afflictions, and to ask ourselves whether we ought to oppose our Father's will, or to distrust his love. We were bound with the cords of our iniquities, with the yoke of our transgressions. Christ, being made a sin-offering for us, to free us from those bonds, himself submitted to be bound for us. To his bonds we owe our liberty; thus the Son makes us free.
18.m:
Sin started in the garden of Eden. That’s where the curse was announced, and where the Redeemer was promised. In another garden, the one who would defeat Satan fought against him. And Jesus was buried in a garden too.

When you’re walking through your garden, think about Jesus’ suffering in a garden. 

Our Lord Jesus knew everything that was going to happen to him. So he asked, “Who are you looking for?” When people tried to make him their king before, he left. But now, when they wanted to force him onto a cross, he willingly gave himself up. He came to earth to suffer and then go to heaven to rule.

Jesus showed what he could have done. He could have knocked everyone down, but he didn’t. Something powerful must have stopped the soldiers and guards from arresting the disciples even though they resisted.

Jesus taught us to be gentle while suffering and to accept whatever happens because it’s God’s plan. The pain is just a little cup, a small thing. It’s a gift from our heavenly Father, who loves us and won’t harm us. 

From Jesus, we learn to accept smaller troubles and wonder if we should question God’s plans or doubt His love. Before, we were trapped by our sins. But Jesus took on those sins to free us. He allowed himself to be tied up for us. Because of his sacrifice, we are free.

18.o: Simon Peter denied his Master. The particulars have been noticed in the remarks on the other Gospels. The beginning of sin is as the letting forth of water. The sin of lying is a fruitful sin; one lie needs another to support it, and that another. If a call to expose ourselves to danger be clear, we may hope God will enable us to honour him; if it be not, we may fear that God will leave us to shame ourselves. They said nothing concerning the miracles of Jesus, by which he had done so much good, and which proved his doctrine. Thus the enemies of Christ, whilst they quarrel with his truth, wilfully shut their eyes against it. He appeals to those who heard him. The doctrine of Christ may safely appeal to all that know it, and those who judge in truth bear witness to it. Our resentment of injuries must never be passionate. He reasoned with the man that did him the injury, and so may we.
18.m:
Peter denied knowing Jesus. We covered the details in the comments about the other Gospels. Sin starts small, like opening a floodgate. Lying leads to more lies because each one needs support. When God clearly calls us to face danger for Him, we can trust He'll help us honor Him. But if the call isn't clear, we might end up shaming ourselves.

They didn’t mention Jesus’ miracles, even though He did so much good through them. His enemies ignored these proofs of His teachings. Jesus asked those who heard Him to confirm what He taught. Anyone who knows the truth about Christ’s message can stand behind it.

We should never let our anger control how we respond to being hurt. Instead, talk calmly with the person who wronged you.

18.o: It was unjust to put one to death who had done so much good, therefore the Jews were willing to save themselves from reproach. Many fear the scandal of an ill thing, more than the sin of it. Christ had said he should be delivered to the Gentiles, and they should put him to death; hereby that saying was fulfilled. He had said that he should be crucified, lifted up. If the Jews had judged him by their law, he had been stoned; crucifying never was used among the Jews. It is determined concerning us, though not discovered to us, what death we shall die: this should free us from disquiet about that matter. Lord, what, when, and how, thou hast appointed.
18.m:
It wasn't fair to punish someone who did so much good, so the Jews wanted to avoid getting a bad reputation. Many people care more about looking bad than actually doing wrong things. Jesus had predicted he would be handed over to non-Jewish rulers, who would kill him. This prediction came true now. He also said he would be crucified and raised up on a cross. If the Jews had followed their own laws, they would have stoned him to death instead of crucifying him, since crucifixion wasn’t part of Jewish punishment. 

Whatever kind of death God has planned for us will happen, even if we don't know all the details. We shouldn't worry about how we'll die. Lord, you decide what, when, and how it happens.

18.o: Art thou the King of the Jews? that King of the Jews who has been so long expected? Messiah the Prince; art thou he? Dost thou call thyself so, and wouldest thou be thought so? Christ answered this question with another; not for evasion, but that Pilate might consider what he did. He never took upon him any earthly power, never were any traitorous principles or practices laid to him. Christ gave an account of the nature of his kingdom. Its nature is not worldly; it is a kingdom within men, set up in their hearts and consciences; its riches spiritual, its power spiritual, and it glory within. Its supports are not worldly; its weapons are spiritual; it needed not, nor used, force to maintain and advance it, nor opposed any kingdom but that of sin and Satan. Its object and design are not worldly. When Christ said, I am the Truth, he said, in effect, I am a King. He conquers by the convincing evidence of truth; he rules by the commanding power of truth. The subjects of this kingdom are those that are of the truth. Pilate put a good question, he said, What is truth? When we search the Scriptures, and attend the ministry of the word, it must be with this inquiry, What is truth? and with this prayer, Lead me in thy truth; into all truth. But many put this question, who have not patience to preserve in their search after truth; or not humility enough to receive it. By this solemn declaration of Christ's innocence, it appears, that though the Lord Jesus was treated as the worst of evil-doers, he never deserved such treatment. But it unfolds the design of his death; that he died as a Sacrifice for our sins. Pilate was willing to please all sides; and was governed more by worldly wisdom than by the rules of justice. Sin is a robber, yet is foolishly chosen by many rather than Christ, who would truly enrich us. Let us endeavour to make our accusers ashamed as Christ did; and let us beware of crucifying Christ afresh.
18.m:
Are you the King of the Jews? The one everyone has been waiting for? The Messiah? Are you saying you are the king?

Jesus didn't directly answer Pilate’s question. Instead, he asked another question. This wasn’t to avoid answering, but to get Pilate thinking about what kind of king he really was.

Jesus explained that his kingdom isn’t like any earthly kingdom. It’s inside people’s hearts and minds. His wealth, power, and honor aren’t things you can see. He doesn’t need armies or money to keep his kingdom strong. He only fights against the kingdoms of sin and Satan.

When Jesus called himself the Truth, he meant he is a king. He wins over people with the power of truth. People follow him because they believe the truth.

Pilate asked what truth means. We should ask ourselves the same thing when we study the Bible or listen to church teachings. We should pray, “Show me the truth.”

But some people just don’t stick around long enough to find out what the truth is. Or they’re too proud to accept it.

By declaring Jesus innocent, we learn that even though he was treated like a criminal, he never did anything wrong. He died to pay for our sins.

Pilate tried to satisfy everyone, but he cared more about staying popular than being fair. Sinners often choose lies over the truth found in Jesus. 

Let’s try to shame our critics by living like Jesus did. And let’s be careful not to reject him again.

19.o: Little did Pilate think with what holy regard these sufferings of Christ would, in after-ages, be thought upon and spoken of by the best and greatest of men. Our Lord Jesus came forth, willing to be exposed to their scorn. It is good for every one with faith, to behold Christ Jesus in his sufferings. Behold him, and love him; be still looking unto Jesus. Did their hatred sharpen their endeavours against him? and shall not our love for him quicken our endeavours for him and his kingdom? Pilate seems to have thought that Jesus might be some person above the common order. Even natural conscience makes men afraid of being found fighting against God. As our Lord suffered for the sins both of Jews and Gentiles, it was a special part of the counsel of Divine Wisdom, that the Jews should first purpose his death, and the Gentiles carry that purpose into effect. Had not Christ been thus rejected of men, we had been for ever rejected of God. Now was the Son of man delivered into the hands of wicked and unreasonable men. He was led forth for us, that we might escape. He was nailed to the cross, as a Sacrifice bound to the altar. The Scripture was fulfilled; he did not die at the altar among the sacrifices, but among criminals sacrificed to public justice. And now let us pause, and with faith look upon Jesus. Was ever sorrow like unto his sorrow? See him bleeding, see him dying, see him and love him! love him, and live to him!
19.m:
Pilate didn't realize how much people in future times would honor the suffering of Jesus. When Jesus came out, he was ready to face their insults. Everyone who believes should look at Jesus during his suffering. Look at him and love him; keep looking at Jesus. 

Did their hate make them work harder against him? Shouldn’t our love for him make us work harder for him and his kingdom?

Pilate seemed to think Jesus was someone important. Natural feelings tell us to avoid fighting against God. Since Jesus died for the sins of both Jews and Gentiles, it made sense that the Jews planned his death and the Romans carried it out.

If Christ hadn’t been rejected by people, we would forever be rejected by God. Now the Son of Man was handed over to evil and unreasonable people. He was led away for us so we could be saved. Nailed to the cross, he became a sacrifice.

The Scriptures were fulfilled; he didn’t die among the regular sacrifices but alongside criminals. Let’s stop and look at Jesus with faith. Has anyone ever felt such sadness as he did?

See him bleeding, see him dying. Look at him and love him! Love him and live for him!

19.o: Here are some remarkable circumstances of Jesus' death, more fully related than before. Pilate would not gratify the chief priests by allowing the writing to be altered; which was doubtless owing to a secret power of God upon his heart, that this statement of our Lord's character and authority might continue. Many things done by the Roman soldiers were fulfilments of the prophecies of the Old Testament. All things therein written shall be fulfilled. Christ tenderly provided for his mother at his death. Sometimes, when God removes one comfort from us, he raises up another for us, where we looked not for it. Christ's example teaches all men to honour their parents in life and death; to provide for their wants, and to promote their comfort by every means in their power. Especially observe the dying word wherewith Jesus breathed out his soul. It is finished; that is, the counsels of the Father concerning his sufferings were now fulfilled. It is finished; all the types and prophecies of the Old Testament, which pointed at the sufferings of the Messiah, were accomplished. It is finished; the ceremonial law is abolished; the substance is now come, and all the shadows are done away. It is finished; an end is made of transgression by bringing in an everlasting righteousness. His sufferings were now finished, both those of his soul, and those of his body. It is finished; the work of man's redemption and salvation is now completed. His life was not taken from him by force, but freely given up.
19.m:
Here are some important details about Jesus' death, explained more fully than before. 

Pilate wouldn't let the chief priests change the sign on the cross, showing respect for Jesus. This was likely because God influenced Pilate’s decision so that the truth about Jesus could be known.

The actions of the Roman soldiers matched what was predicted in the Old Testament. Everything written there came true.

Jesus took care of his mother Mary just before he died. When God takes away one comfort, He often provides another we don’t expect.

Christ set an example for everyone to honor their parents, taking care of them and making sure they’re comfortable.

Notice what Jesus said right before he died: “It is finished.” This means God’s plan for Jesus’ suffering was complete. 

“It is finished” also means all the predictions about Jesus in the Old Testament had been fulfilled. 

“It is finished” shows that the old laws have ended, and the new covenant has begun. 

“It is finished” means sin is over, and eternal righteousness has started. 

Both the physical and spiritual suffering of Jesus were now over. 

“It is finished” means the work of saving people is done. 

Jesus didn’t die by force; he willingly gave up his life.

19.o: A trial was made whether Jesus was dead. He died in less time than persons crucified commonly did. It showed that he had laid down his life of himself. The spear broke up the very fountains of life; no human body could survive such a wound. But its being so solemnly attested, shows there was something peculiar in it. The blood and water that flowed out, signified those two great benefits which all believers partake of through Christ, justification and sanctification; blood for atonement, water for purification. They both flow from the pierced side of our Redeemer. To Christ crucified we owe merit for our justification, and Spirit and grace for our sanctification. Let this silence the fears of weak Christians, and encourage their hopes; there came both water and blood out of Jesus' pierced side, both to justify and sanctify them. The Scripture was fulfilled, in Pilate's not allowing his legs to be broken, Ps 34:20. There was a type of this in the paschal lamb, Ex 12:46. May we ever look to Him, whom, by our sins, we have ignorantly and heedlessly pierced, nay, sometimes against convictions and mercies; and who shed from his wounded side both water and blood, that we might be justified and sanctified in his name.
19.m:
They checked if Jesus was really dead. Usually, people on crosses took longer to die, but Jesus died quickly. This showed that he gave up his life willingly.

The soldier stabbed him with a spear, causing blood and water to come out. This proved Jesus was truly dead. 

Blood represents forgiveness for sins, while water stands for becoming clean inside. Both these gifts come to us because Jesus was hurt. We get forgiveness through what Jesus did on the cross, and God’s help to live right comes from Jesus’ sacrifice.

This should comfort anyone worried about their faith. When Jesus was hurt, both blood and water poured out—both to forgive sins and make us holy.

The soldiers didn’t break Jesus’ legs, just like an old prophecy said they wouldn't, Psalm 34:20. Also, the Passover lamb wasn’t supposed to have its bones broken, Exodus 12:46.

We should always turn to Jesus, even though we’ve hurt him without knowing it or ignored his love before. Because of the wounds in his side, he gives us both forgiveness and strength to live good lives.

19.o: Joseph of Arimathea was a disciple of Christ in secret. Disciples should openly own themselves; yet some, who in lesser trials have been fearful, in greater have been courageous. When God has work to do, he can find out such as are proper to do it. The embalming was done by Nicodemus, a secret friend to Christ, though not his constant follower. That grace which at first is like a bruised reed, may afterward resemble a strong cedar. Hereby these two rich men showed the value they had for Christ's person and doctrine, and that it was not lessened by the reproach of the cross. We must do our duty as the present day and opportunity are, and leave it to God to fulfil his promises in his own way and his own time. The grave of Jesus was appointed with the wicked, as was the case of those who suffered as criminals; but he was with the rich in his death, as prophesied, Isa 53:9; these two circumstances it was very unlikely should ever be united in the same person. He was buried in a new sepulchre; therefore it could not be said that it was not he, but some other that rose. We also are here taught not to be particular as to the place of our burial. He was buried in the sepulchre next at hand. Here is the Sun of Righteousness set for a while, to rise again in greater glory, and then to set no more.
19.m:
Joseph of Arimathea was a follower of Christ in secret. Followers should be open about their faith, but sometimes people who were afraid before become brave when faced with bigger challenges. 

When God needs help, he finds the right people. Nicodemus, another secret supporter of Christ, helped with the burial. Sometimes what starts weak becomes strong over time. These two wealthy men showed how much they valued Christ’s teachings even after facing shame from his crucifixion. 

We should do what we can now and trust God to fulfill his promises later. Jesus was put in a tomb meant for criminals, just like the prophecy said. Yet, he was also placed near rich people’s tombs, which was unusual. 

Jesus was buried in a new tomb so no one could claim someone else rose instead. It doesn’t matter where we’re buried. Jesus was laid to rest nearby. This shows us that the sun of righteousness sets temporarily but rises again with even greater glory.

20.o: If Christ gave his life a ransom, and had not taken it again, it would not have appeared that his giving it was accepted as satisfaction. It was a great trial to Mary, that the body was gone. Weak believers often make that the matter of complaint, which is really just ground of hope, and matter of joy. It is well when those more honoured than others with the privileges of disciples, are more active than others in the duty of disciples; more willing to take pains, and run hazards, in a good work. We must do our best, and neither envy those who can do better, nor despise those who do as well as they can, though they come behind. The disciple whom Jesus loved in a special manner, and who therefore in a special manner loved Jesus, was foremost. The love of Christ will make us to abound in every duty more than any thing else. He that was behind was Peter, who had denied Christ. A sense of guilt hinders us in the service of God. As yet the disciples knew not the Scripture; they Christ must rise again from the dead.
20.m:
If Christ gave his life as a payment and did not take it back, it wouldn’t show that his sacrifice was accepted. 

It was hard on Mary that the body was missing. Sometimes weak believers complain about things that should actually give them hope and joy.

Those who get more honor as followers of Jesus usually try harder at their duties. They’re willing to put in effort and face risks for what’s right.

We should do our best without envying those who can do better or looking down on those who do as much as they can, even if they don’t measure up.

The disciple Jesus loved most and who loved Jesus most was the first one there.

When we truly love Christ, we’ll be eager to serve him in every way.

Peter, who had denied Christ, was the one lagging behind because he felt guilty.

At that time, the disciples didn’t know the Scriptures. They didn’t realize that Christ would rise from the dead.

20.o: We are likely to seek and find, when we seek with affection, and seek in tears. But many believers complain of the clouds and darkness they are under, which are methods of grace for humbling their souls, mortifying their sins, and endearing Christ to them. A sight of angels and their smiles, will not suffice, without a sight of Jesus, and God's smiles in him. None know, but those who have tasted it, the sorrows of a deserted soul, which has had comfortable evidences of the love of God in Christ, and hopes of heaven, but has now lost them, and walks in darkness; such a wounded spirit who can bear? Christ, in manifesting himself to those that seek him, often outdoes their expectations. See how Mary's heart was in earnest to find Jesus. Christ's way of making himself known to his people is by his word; his word applied to their souls, speaking to them in particular. It might be read, Is it my Master? See with what pleasure those who love Jesus speak of his authority over them. He forbids her to expect that his bodily presence look further, than the present state of things. Observe the relation to God, from union with Christ. We, partaking of a Divine nature, Christ's Father is our Father; and he, partaking of the human nature, our God is his God. Christ's ascension into heaven, there to plead for us, is likewise an unspeakable comfort. Let them not think this earth is to be their home and rest; their eye and aim, and earnest desires, must be upon another world, and this ever upon their hearts, I ascend, therefore I must seek the things which are above. And let those who know the word of Christ, endeavour that others should get good from their knowledge.
20.m:
When we search for something with real feeling and even cry about it, we're more likely to find what we're looking for. Many believers feel sad because they’re in dark times meant to humble them, kill off their bad habits, and bring them closer to Christ. Seeing angels smile isn’t enough if you don't see Jesus and God’s smile on him. Only those who’ve felt it know how painful it is to lose comforting proof of God’s love through Christ and hope of heaven. When Jesus shows himself to those seeking him, he often goes beyond their wildest dreams. Look at how seriously Mary wanted to find Jesus. 

Jesus lets people know he’s around mainly through his words—his message reaching deep inside them. She could have asked, “Is that really my Master?” Those who love Jesus talk happily about his power over them. Jesus tells her not to expect him back in physical form anytime soon. Notice how being joined to Christ makes God our Father too, and since Christ took on human nature, our God is his God. 

Christ going up to heaven to pray for us gives us great joy. Don’t think Earth is where we belong forever. Our focus needs to always be on heaven, saying, “I’m going up, so keep your eyes on higher things.” People who know Christ’s teachings should help others learn too.

20.o: This was the first day of the week, and this day is afterwards often mentioned by the sacred writers; for it was evidently set apart as the Christian sabbath, in remembrance of Christ's resurrection. The disciples had shut the doors for fear of the Jews; and when they had no such expectation, Jesus himself came and stood in the midst of them, having miraculously, though silently, opened the doors. It is a comfort to Christ's disciples, when their assemblies can only be held in private, that no doors can shut out Christ's presence. When He manifests his love to believers by the comforts of his Spirit, he assures them that because he lives, they shall live also. A sight of Christ will gladden the heart of a disciple at any time; and the more we see of Jesus, the more we shall rejoice. He said, Receive ye the Holy Ghost, thus showing that their spiritual life, as well as all their ability for their work, would be derived from him, and depended upon him. Every word of Christ which is received in the heart by faith, comes accompanied by this Divine breathing; and without this there is neither light nor life. Nothing is seen, known, discerned, or felt of God, but through this. After this, Christ directed the apostles to declare the only method by which sin would be forgiven. This power did not exist at all in the apostles as a power to give judgment, but only as a power to declare the character of those whom God would accept or reject in the day of judgment. They have clearly laid down the marks whereby a child of God may be discerned and be distinguished from a false professor; and according to what they have declared shall every case be decided in the day of judgment. When we assemble in Christ's name, especially on his holy day, he will meet with us, and speak peace to us. The disciples of Christ should endeavour to build up one another in their most holy faith, both by repeating what they have heard to those that were absent, and by making known what they have experienced. Thomas limited the Holy One of Israel, when he would be convinced by his own method or not at all. He might justly have been left in his unbelief, after rejecting such abundant proofs. The fears and sorrows of the disciples are often lengthened, to punish their negligence.
20.m:
This was the first day of the week, and later writers often mention this day as the Christian Sabbath, remembering Christ’s resurrection. 

The disciples had locked the doors out of fear of the Jews. But even so, Jesus appeared among them, opening the doors in some miraculous way that didn’t make a sound. 

It’s comforting to know that nothing can keep Christ away from his followers, even if they’re meeting privately. When he shows his love through the Spirit’s comfort, he reminds them that since he lives, they will live too. Seeing Christ always brings joy to his followers. The more we see of Jesus, the happier we become. 

He told them, “Receive the Holy Spirit.” This means that everything about their spiritual life and ability to do their work comes from him. Each word of Christ, accepted by faith, brings the Holy Spirit. Without the Spirit, there is no understanding or life. We cannot see, know, or feel anything about God except through the Spirit.

Then, Christ instructed the apostles to tell people the only way sins are forgiven. This wasn’t a power to judge others, but to explain who God accepts or rejects on Judgment Day. They described true believers and false ones, setting guidelines used to decide each person’s fate on Judgment Day.

When we gather in Christ’s name, especially on his special day, he meets us and gives us peace. Followers of Christ should help each other grow stronger in faith by sharing what they’ve learned and experienced.

Thomas wanted proof before believing, limiting the Holy One of Israel. He could have stayed in doubt despite all the evidence, but thankfully, Jesus gave him the chance to believe.

The disciples' fears and sadness often last longer to punish their carelessness.

20.o: That one day in seven should be religiously observed, was an appointment from the beginning. And that, in the kingdom of the Messiah, the first day of the week should be that solemn day, was pointed out, in that Christ on that day once and again met his disciples in a religious assembly. The religious observance of that day has come down to us through every age of the church. There is not an unbelieving word in our tongues, nor thought in our minds, but it is known to the Lord Jesus; and he was pleased to accommodate himself even to Thomas, rather than leave him in his unbelief. We ought thus to bear with the weak, Ro 15:1, 2. This warning is given to all. If we are faithless, we are Christless and graceless, hopeless and joyless. Thomas was ashamed of his unbelief, and cried out, My Lord and my God. He spoke with affection, as one that took hold of Christ with all his might; “My Lord and my God.” Sound and sincere believers, though slow and weak, shall be graciously accepted of the Lord Jesus. It is the duty of those who read and hear the gospel, to believe, to embrace the doctrine of Christ, and that record concerning him, 1Jo 5:11.
20.m:
Every seventh day should be set aside for worship. In the time of Jesus, the first day of the week became the special day for Christians to gather. People have kept this tradition since then.

Jesus knows everything about us. Even if someone doubts, like Thomas did, Jesus still reaches out to them. We should be patient with people who struggle to believe. 

Thomas felt bad about doubting before. Then he said, "My Lord and my God." He showed real love by holding onto Jesus tightly. Slow learners and weak believers will still be welcomed by Jesus.

Those who listen to the gospel should trust what it teaches about Jesus. They need to accept the truth about him.

20.o: There were other signs and proofs of our Lord's resurrection, but these were committed to writing, that all might believe that Jesus was the promised Messiah, the Saviour of sinners, and the Son of God; that, by this faith, they might obtain eternal life, by his mercy, truth, and power. May we believe that Jesus is the Christ, and believing may we have life through his name.
20.m: There were other signs and proofs of our Lord’s resurrection, but these were written down so everyone could believe that Jesus was the promised Messiah, the Savior of sinners, and the Son of God. By having faith in him, people could receive eternal life because of his mercy, truth, and power. Let us believe that Jesus is the Christ, and by believing, let us gain life through his name.

21.o: Christ makes himself known to his people, usually in his ordinances; but sometimes by his Spirit he visits them when employed in their business. It is good for the disciples of Christ to be together in common conversation, and common business. The hour for their entering upon action was not come. They would help to maintain themselves, and not be burdensome to any. Christ's time of making himself known to his people, is when they are most at a loss. He knows the temporal wants of his people, and has promised them not only grace sufficient, but food convenient. Divine Providence extends itself to things most minute, and those are happy who acknowledge God in all their ways. Those who are humble, diligent, and patient, though their labours may be crossed, shall be crowned; they sometimes live to see their affairs take a happy turn, after many struggles. And there is nothing lost by observing Christ's orders; it is casting the net on the right side of the ship. Jesus manifests himself to his people by doing that for them which none else can do, and things which they looked not for. He would take care that those who left all for him, should not want any good thing. And latter favours are to bring to mind former favours, that eaten bread may not be forgotten. He whom Jesus loved was the first that said, It is the Lord. John had cleaved most closely to his Master in his sufferings, and knew him soonest. Peter was the most zealous, and reached Christ the first. How variously God dispenses his gifts, and what difference there may be between some believers and others in the way of their honouring Christ, yet they all may be accepted of him! Others continue in the ship, drag the net, and bring the fish to shore, and such persons ought not to be blamed as worldly; for they, in their places, are as truly serving Christ as the others. The Lord Jesus had provision ready for them. We need not be curious in inquiring whence this came; but we may be comforted at Christ's care for his disciples. Although there were so many, and such great fishes, yet they lost none, nor damaged their net. The net of the gospel has enclosed multitudes, yet it is as strong as ever to bring souls to God.
21.m:
Christ often shows himself to his followers through his teachings and commands. Sometimes, he reveals himself even while they go about their daily tasks. 

It’s good for Christians to spend time together, talking and working together. But the time wasn’t right yet for them to start their mission. They needed to support themselves without being a burden to anyone.

When people feel unsure, Christ appears to them. He cares about their needs and promises to provide enough for them. God watches over even small details, and those who recognize God in everything are blessed.

Those who work hard and stay patient will eventually succeed, even if they face challenges along the way. Their efforts pay off in the end.

Following Christ’s instructions leads to success. Casting the net on the right side of the boat brought results.

Jesus proves himself to his followers by doing things only he can do. He takes care of those who leave everything for him, ensuring they don't lack anything important.

Later blessings remind us of earlier ones, helping us remember God’s goodness.

The disciple closest to Jesus recognized him first. Peter was eager and reached out to Christ quickly.

God gives different gifts to different believers, and all are valued by Christ.

Some stayed in the boat, pulling in the nets and bringing in the fish. These actions too serve Christ faithfully.

Jesus provided for them already. We shouldn’t wonder where the meal came from but be glad Christ took care of them.

Even with many big fish, they didn’t lose any or break their nets. This shows how well the church gathers many people without losing its strength.

21.o: Our Lord addressed Peter by his original name, as if he had forfeited that of Peter through his denying him. He now answered, Thou knowest that I love thee; but without professing to love Jesus more than others. We must not be surprised to have our sincerity called into question, when we ourselves have done that which makes it doubtful. Every remembrance of past sins, even pardoned sins, renews the sorrow of a true penitent. Conscious of integrity, Peter solemnly appealed to Christ, as knowing all things, even the secrets of his heart. It is well when our falls and mistakes make us more humble and watchful. The sincerity of our love to God must be brought to the test; and it behoves us to inquire with earnest, preserving prayer to the heart-searching God, to examine and prove us, whether we are able to stand this test. No one can be qualified to feed the sheep and lambs of Christ, who does not love the good Shepherd more than any earthly advantage or object. It is the great concern of every good man, whatever death he dies, to glorify God in it; for what is our chief end but this, to die to the Lord, at the word of the Lord?
21.m:
Our Lord spoke to Peter using his old name, Simon, because Peter had denied him. Peter then said, "You know that I love you." But he did not claim to love Jesus more than anyone else. 

We shouldn't be shocked when people doubt our honesty after we've done something wrong. Reminding ourselves of past mistakes often brings deep regret. 

Since Peter knew he was honest, he asked Jesus, who knows everything, to confirm it. When we mess up, we should become more humble and careful. 

The truth of our love for God needs testing. We need to pray sincerely, asking God to search our hearts and see if we're truly ready for such tests. 

No one can lead Christ’s flock unless they love Jesus more than anything else in life. 

It's important for each of us to honor God, no matter how we might die. Our main goal is always to live and die for the Lord.

21.o: Sufferings, pains, and death, will appear formidable even to the experienced Christian; but in the hope to glorify God, to leave a sinful world, and to be present with his Lord, he becomes ready to obey the Redeemer's call, and to follow Him through death to glory. It is the will of Christ that his disciples should mind their own duty, and not be curious about future events, either as to themselves or others. Many things we are apt to be anxious about, which are nothing to us. Other people's affairs are nothing to us, to intermeddle in; we must quietly work, and mind our own business. Many curious questions are put about the counsels of God, and the state of the unseen world, as to which we may say, What is this to us? And if we attend to the duty of following Christ, we shall find neither heart nor time to meddle with that which does not belong to us. How little are any unwritten traditions to be relied upon! Let the Scripture be its own interpreter, and explain itself; as it is, in a great measure, its own evidence, and proves itself, for it is light. See the easy setting right such mistakes by the word of Christ. Scripture language is the safest channel for Scripture truth; the words which the Holy Ghost teaches, 1Co 2:13. Those who cannot agree in the same terms of art, and the application of them, may yet agree in the same Scripture terms, and to love one another.
21.m:
Suffering, pain, and death can seem scary even to someone who has faith. But knowing that these things will help honor God, escape sin, and be with Jesus makes a believer willing to follow Jesus through death to heaven.

Jesus wants his followers to focus on doing what’s right instead of worrying too much about the future. There are many things we tend to worry about that don’t really matter. We shouldn't get involved in other people's problems. We need to stay focused on our own duties.

People often ask detailed questions about God's plans and life after death. For those kinds of questions, we can simply say, “What does that have to do with me?” When we concentrate on following Jesus, we won’t have time to think about things that aren’t important.

We shouldn’t trust stories passed down without written proof. The best way to know the truth is from the Bible, which explains itself. Jesus corrects misunderstandings using the Scriptures.

The safest way to learn the truth is through the Bible. The Spirit guides us as we read the words He inspired. People might use different words to describe ideas, but they can still agree on the Bible's teachings and love each other.

21.o: Only a small part of the actions of Jesus had been written. But let us bless God for all that is in the Scriptures, and be thankful that there is so much in so small a space. Enough is recorded to direct our faith, and regulate our practice; more would have been unnecessary. Much of what is written is overlooked, much forgotten, and much made the matter of doubtful disputes. We may, however, look forward to the joy we shall receive in heaven, from a more complete knowledge of all Jesus did and said, as well as of the conduct of his providence and grace in his dealings with each of us. May this be our happiness. These are written that ye might believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God; and that believing ye might have life through his name, ch. 20:31.
21.m:
Only a small part of Jesus' actions were written down. Let’s thank God for everything in the scriptures, even though there isn’t much. What is written helps guide our faith and behavior. More details wouldn't have been needed anyway. 

A lot of what was written gets ignored or forgotten, and some parts cause arguments. But one day in heaven, we will know all about Jesus’ words and deeds. That will bring us great joy.

These writings help us believe that Jesus is the Messiah, the Son of God. And by believing, we gain eternal life through him, chapter 20:31.
